name: LADR_def_chap_1_1
nl_statement:
\begin{itemize}
  \item A \emph{complex number} is an ordered pair \((a, b)\), where \(a, b \in \mathbb{R}\), but we will write this as \(a + bi\).
  \item The set of all complex numbers is denoted by \(\mathbb{C}\):
  \[
    \mathbb{C} = \{ a + bi : a, b \in \mathbb{R} \}.
  \]
  \item \emph{Addition} and \emph{multiplication} on \(\mathbb{C}\) are defined by
  \[
    (a + bi) + (c + di) = (a + c) + (b + d)i,
  \]
  \[
    (a + bi)(c + di) = (ac - bd) + (ad + bc)i;
  \]
  here \(a, b, c, d \in \mathbb{R}\).
\end{itemize}

name: LADR_def_chap_1_5
nl_statement:
Suppose \(\alpha, \beta \in \mathbf{C}\).
\begin{itemize}
  \item Let \(-\alpha\) denote the additive inverse of \(\alpha\). Thus \(-\alpha\) is the unique complex number such that
  \[
    \alpha + (-\alpha) = 0.
  \]
  \item \emph{Subtraction} on \(\mathbf{C}\) is defined by
  \[
    \beta - \alpha = \beta + (-\alpha).
  \]
  \item For \(\alpha \neq 0\), let \(1/\alpha\) and \(\frac{1}{\alpha}\) denote the multiplicative inverse of \(\alpha\). Thus \(1/\alpha\) is the unique complex number such that
  \[
    \alpha(1/\alpha) = 1.
  \]
  \item For \(\alpha \neq 0\), \emph{division} by \(\alpha\) is defined by
  \[
    \beta/\alpha = \beta(1/\alpha).
  \]
\end{itemize}

name: LADR_def_chap_1_8
LADR_def_chap_1_11
nl_statement:
\(\mathbf{F}^n\) is the set of all lists of length \(n\) of elements of \(\mathbf{F}\):
\[
\mathbf{F}^n = \{(x_1, \ldots, x_n) : x_k \in \mathbf{F} \text{ for } k = 1, \ldots, n\}.
\]
For \((x_1, \ldots, x_n) \in \mathbf{F}^n\) and \(k \in \{1, \ldots, n\}\), we say that \(x_k\) is the \(k^{\text{th}}\) \emph{coordinate} of \((x_1, \ldots, x_n)\).

name: LADR_def_chap_1_13
nl_statement:
\emph{Addition} in \(\mathbb{F}^n\) is defined by adding corresponding coordinates:
\[
(x_1, \ldots, x_n) + (y_1, \ldots, y_n) = (x_1 + y_1, \ldots, x_n + y_n).
\]

name: LADR_def_chap_1_17
nl_statement:
For \(x \in \mathbb{F}^n\), the \emph{additive inverse} of \(x\), denoted by \(-x\), is the vector \(-x \in \mathbb{F}^n\) such that
\[
x + (-x) = 0.
\]
Thus if \(x = (x_1, \ldots, x_n)\), then \(-x = (-x_1, \ldots, -x_n)\).

name: LADR_def_chap_1_18
nl_statement:
The \emph{product} of a number \(\lambda\) and a vector in \(\mathbb{F}^n\) is computed by multiplying each coordinate of the vector by \(\lambda\):
\[
\lambda(x_1, \ldots, x_n) = (\lambda x_1, \ldots, \lambda x_n);
\]
here \(\lambda \in \mathbb{F}\) and \((x_1, \ldots, x_n) \in \mathbb{F}^n\).

name: LADR_def_chap_1_19
LADR_def_chap_1_20
nl_statement:
A \emph{vector space} is a set \(V\) along with an addition on \(V\) and a scalar multiplication on \(V\) such that the following properties hold.
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{commutativity} \\
  \(u + v = v + u\) for all \(u, v \in V\).
  
  \item \textbf{associativity} \\
  \((u + v) + w = u + (v + w)\) and \((ab)v = a(bv)\) for all \(u, v, w \in V\) and for all \(a, b \in \mathbb{F}\).

  \item \textbf{additive identity} \\
  There exists an element \(0 \in V\) such that \(v + 0 = v\) for all \(v \in V\).

  \item \textbf{additive inverse} \\
  For every \(v \in V\), there exists \(w \in V\) such that \(v + w = 0\).

  \item \textbf{multiplicative identity} \\
  \(1v = v\) for all \(v \in V\).

  \item \textbf{distributive properties} \\
  \(a(u + v) = au + av\) and \((a + b)v = av + bv\) for all \(a, b \in \mathbb{F}\) and all \(u, v \in V\).
\end{itemize}

name: LADR_def_chap_1_21
nl_statement: Elements of a vector space are called \emph{vectors} or \emph{points}.

name: LADR_def_chap_1_22
LADR_def_chap_1_24
nl_statement:
\begin{itemize}
  \item If $S$ is a set, then $\mathbf{F}^S$ denotes the set of functions from $S$ to $\mathbf{F}$.
  \item For $f, g \in \mathbf{F}^S$, the \textit{sum} $f + g \in \mathbf{F}^S$ is the function defined by
  \[
  (f + g)(x) = f(x) + g(x)
  \]
  for all $x \in S$.
  \item For $\lambda \in \mathbf{F}$ and $f \in \mathbf{F}^S$, the \textit{product} $\lambda f \in \mathbf{F}^S$ is the function defined by
  \[
  (\lambda f)(x) = \lambda f(x)
  \]
  for all $x \in S$.
\end{itemize}

name: LADR_def_chap_1_33
nl_statement: A subset $U$ of $V$ is called a \textit{subspace} of $V$ if $U$ is also a vector space with the same additive identity, addition, and scalar multiplication as on $V$.

name: LADR_def_chap_1_36
nl_statement:
Suppose $V_1, \ldots, V_m$ are subspaces of $V$. The \textit{sum} of $V_1, \ldots, V_m$, denoted by $V_1 + \cdots + V_m$, is the set of all possible sums of elements of $V_1, \ldots, V_m$. More precisely,
\[
V_1 + \cdots + V_m = \{v_1 + \cdots + v_m : v_1 \in V_1, \ldots, v_m \in V_m\}.
\]

name: LADR_def_chap_1_41
LADR_def_chap_2_2
nl_statement:
A \emph{linear combination} of a list \(v_1,\dots,v_m\) of vectors in \(V\) is a vector of the form
\[
  a_1v_1 + \cdots + a_mv_m,
\]
where \(a_1,\dots,a_m \in \mathbb{F}\).

name: LADR_def_chap_2_4
nl_statement:
The set of all linear combinations of a list of vectors \(v_1,\dots,v_m\) in \(V\) is called the \emph{span} of \(v_1,\dots,v_m\), denoted by \(\operatorname{span}(v_1,\dots,v_m)\). In other words,
\[
  \operatorname{span}(v_1,\dots,v_m)
  = \{\,a_1v_1 + \cdots + a_mv_m : a_1,\dots,a_m \in \mathbb{F}\}.
\]
The span of the empty list \((\,)\) is defined to be \(\{0\}\).

name: LADR_def_chap_2_7
nl_statement: If \(\operatorname{span}(v_1,\dots,v_m)=V\), we say that the list \(v_1,\dots,v_m\) \emph{spans} \(V\).

name: LADR_def_chap_2_9
nl_statement: A vector space is called \emph{finite-dimensional} if some list of vectors in it spans the space.

name: LADR_def_chap_2_10
nl_statement:
\begin{itemize}
  \item A function \(p: \mathbb{F} \to \mathbb{F}\) is called a \emph{polynomial} with coefficients in \(\mathbb{F}\) if there exist \(a_0,\dots,a_m \in \mathbb{F}\) such that
  \[
    p(z) = a_0 + a_1z + a_2z^2 + \cdots + a_mz^m
    \quad\text{for all } z \in \mathbb{F}.
  \]
  \item \(\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{F})\) is the set of all polynomials with coefficients in \(\mathbb{F}\).
\end{itemize}

name: LADR_def_chap_2_11
nl_statement:
\begin{itemize}
  \item A polynomial \(p \in \mathcal{P}(\mathbb{F})\) is said to have \emph{degree} \(m\) if there exist scalars \(a_0,\dots,a_m \in \mathbb{F}\) with \(a_m \neq 0\) such that for every \(z \in \mathbb{F}\),
  \[
    p(z) = a_0 + a_1z + \cdots + a_m z^m.
  \]
  \item The polynomial that is identically \(0\) is said to have degree \(-\infty\).
  \item The degree of a polynomial \(p\) is denoted by \(\deg p\).
\end{itemize}

name: LADR_def_chap_2_13
nl_statement: A vector space is called \emph{infinite-dimensional} if it is not finite-dimensional.

name: LADR_def_chap_2_15
nl_statement:
A list \(v_1,\dots,v_m\) of vectors in \(V\) is called \emph{linearly independent} if the only choice of scalars \(a_1,\dots,a_m\in\mathbb{F}\) that makes
\[
  a_1v_1 + \cdots + a_mv_m = 0
\]
is \(a_1 = \cdots = a_m = 0\). The empty list \((\,)\) is also declared to be linearly independent.

name: LADR_def_chap_2_17
nl_statement:
A list of vectors in \(V\) is called \emph{linearly dependent} if it is not linearly independent. In other words, a list \(v_1,\dots,v_m\) of vectors in \(V\) is linearly dependent if there exist scalars \(a_1,\dots,a_m\in\mathbb{F}\), not all zero, such that
\[
  a_1v_1 + \cdots + a_mv_m = 0.
\]

name: LADR_def_chap_2_26
nl_statement: A \emph{basis} of \(V\) is a list of vectors in \(V\) that is linearly independent and spans \(V\).

name: LADR_def_chap_2_35
LADR_def_chap_3_1
nl_statement:
is a function
\[
  T \colon V \to W
\]
with the following properties:
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Additivity.}
    \[
      T(u + v) = Tu + Tv
      \quad\text{for all }u,v\in V.
    \]
  \item \textbf{Homogeneity.}
    \[
      T(\lambda v) = \lambda\,T(v)
      \quad\text{for all }\lambda\in\mathbb{F}\text{ and }v\in V.
    \]
\end{itemize}

name: LADR_def_chap_3_5
nl_statement:
defined by
\[
  (S+T)(v)=Sv+Tv
  \quad\text{and}\quad
  (\lambda T)(v)=\lambda(Tv)
\]
for all \(v\in V\).

name: LADR_def_chap_3_7
nl_statement:
is defined by
\[
  (ST)(u) = S(Tu)
  \quad\text{for all }u\in U.
\]

name: LADR_def_chap_3_11
nl_statement:
:
\[
  \mathrm{null}\,T = \{\,v\in V : T v = 0\}.
\]

name: LADR_def_chap_3_14
nl_statement: A function \(T\colon V \to W\) is called \emph{injective} if \(Tu = Tv\) implies \(u = v\).

name: LADR_def_chap_3_16
nl_statement:
For \(T\in\mathcal{L}(V,W)\), the \emph{range} of \(T\) is the subset of \(W\) consisting of those vectors that are equal to \(T v\) for some \(v\in V\):
\[
  \mathrm{range}\,T = \{\,T v : v\in V\}.
\]

name: LADR_def_chap_3_19
nl_statement: A function \(T\colon V \to W\) is called \emph{surjective} if its range equals \(W\).

name: LADR_def_chap_3_29
nl_statement:
Suppose \(m\) and \(n\) are nonnegative integers. An \(m\)-by-\(n\) matrix \(A\) is a rectangular array of elements of \(\mathbb{F}\) with \(m\) rows and \(n\) columns:
\[
  A = \begin{pmatrix}
    A_{1,1} & \cdots & A_{1,n} \\
    \vdots  & \ddots & \vdots  \\
    A_{m,1} & \cdots & A_{m,n}
  \end{pmatrix}.
\]
The notation \(A_{j,k}\) denotes the entry in row \(j\), column \(k\) of \(A\).

name: LADR_def_chap_3_31
nl_statement:
Suppose \(T\in\mathcal{L}(V,W)\) and \(v_1,\dots,v_n\) is a basis of \(V\) and \(w_1,\dots,w_m\) is a basis of \(W\). The matrix of \(T\) with respect to these bases is the \(m\)-by-\(n\) matrix \(\mathcal{M}(T)\) whose entries \(A_{j,k}\) are defined by
\[
  T v_k = A_{1,k}w_1 + \cdots + A_{m,k}w_m.
\]
If the bases \(v_1,\dots,v_n\) and \(w_1,\dots,w_m\) are not clear from the context, then the notation \(\mathcal{M}(T,(v_1,\dots,v_n),(w_1,\dots,w_m))\) is used.

name: LADR_def_chap_3_34
LADR_def_chap_3_36
LADR_def_chap_3_41
nl_statement:
Suppose \(A\) is an \(m\)-by-\(n\) matrix and \(B\) is an \(n\)-by-\(p\) matrix. Then \(AB\) is defined to be the \(m\)-by-\(p\) matrix whose entry in row \(j\), column \(k\), is given by the equation
\[
  (AB)_{j,k} \;=\; \sum_{r=1}^n A_{j,r}\,B_{r,k}.
\]
Thus the entry in row \(j\), column \(k\), of \(AB\) is computed by taking row \(j\) of \(A\) and column \(k\) of \(B\), multiplying together corresponding entries, and then summing.

name: LADR_def_chap_3_52
LADR_def_chap_3_54
nl_statement:
The \emph{transpose} of a matrix \(A\), denoted by \(A^t\), is the matrix obtained from \(A\) by interchanging rows and columns. Specifically, if \(A\) is an \(m\)-by-\(n\) matrix, then \(A^t\) is the \(n\)-by-\(m\) matrix whose entries are given by the equation
\[
  (A^t)_{k,j} = A_{j,k}.
\]

name: LADR_def_chap_3_58
nl_statement: The rank of a matrix \(A \in \mathbb{F}^{m,n}\) is the column rank of \(A\).

name: LADR_def_chap_3_59
LADR_def_chap_3_69
LADR_def_chap_3_73
nl_statement:
Suppose \(v\in V\) and \(v_1,\dots,v_n\) is a basis of \(V\). The matrix of \(v\) with respect to this basis is the \(n\)-by-1 matrix
\[
  \mathcal{M}(v) = 
  \begin{pmatrix}
    b_1 \\
    \vdots \\
    b_n
  \end{pmatrix},
\]
where \(b_1,\dots,b_n\) are the scalars such that
\[
  v = b_1 v_1 + \cdots + b_n v_n.
\]

name: LADR_def_chap_3_79
nl_statement:
Suppose \(n\) is a positive integer. The \(n\)-by-\(n\) matrix
\[
  \begin{pmatrix}
    1 &       & 0 \\
      & \ddots &   \\
    0 &       & 1
  \end{pmatrix}
\]
with 1’s on the diagonal (the entries where the row number equals the column number) and 0’s elsewhere is called the identity matrix and is denoted by \(I\).

name: LADR_def_chap_3_80
nl_statement:
A square matrix \(A\) is called \emph{invertible} if there is a square matrix \(B\) of the same size such that
\[
  AB = BA = I;
\]
we call \(B\) the \emph{inverse} of \(A\) and denote it by \(A^{-1}\).

name: LADR_def_chap_3_87
nl_statement:
Suppose \(V_1,\dots,V_m\) are vector spaces over \(\mathbb{F}\).
\begin{itemize}
  \item The \emph{product} \(V_1\times\cdots\times V_m\) is defined by
    \[
      V_1\times\cdots\times V_m
      =\{(v_1,\dots,v_m)\colon v_1\in V_1,\dots,v_m\in V_m\}.
    \]
  \item Addition on \(V_1\times\cdots\times V_m\) is defined by
    \[
      (u_1,\dots,u_m)+(v_1,\dots,v_m)
      = (u_1+v_1,\dots,u_m+v_m).
    \]
  \item Scalar multiplication on \(V_1\times\cdots\times V_m\) is defined by
    \[
      \lambda\,(v_1,\dots,v_m)
      = (\lambda v_1,\dots,\lambda v_m).
    \]
\end{itemize}

name: LADR_def_chap_3_97
nl_statement: For \(v \in V\) and \(U \subseteq V\), the set \(v + U\) is said to be a \emph{translate} of \(U\).

name: LADR_def_chap_3_99
nl_statement:
Suppose \(U\) is a subspace of \(V\). Then the \emph{quotient space} \(V/U\) is the set of all translates of \(U\). Thus
\[
  V/U = \{\,v + U : v \in V\}.
\]

name: LADR_def_chap_3_102
nl_statement:
Suppose \(U\) is a subspace of \(V\). Then addition and scalar multiplication are defined on \(V/U\) by
\[
  (v + U) + (w + U) = (v + w) + U,
\]
\[
  \lambda (v + U) = (\lambda v) + U
\]
for all \(v, w \in V\) and all \(\lambda \in \mathbb{F}\).

name: LADR_def_chap_3_104
nl_statement:
Suppose \(U\) is a subspace of \(V\). The \emph{quotient map} \(\pi: V \to V/U\) is the linear map defined by
\[
  \pi(v) = v + U
\]
for each \(v \in V\).

name: LADR_def_chap_3_108
nl_statement: A \emph{linear functional} on \(V\) is a linear map from \(V\) to \(\mathbb{F}\). In other words, a linear functional is an element of \(\mathcal{L}(V,\mathbb{F})\).

name: LADR_def_chap_3_110
nl_statement:
The dual space of \(V\), denoted by \(V'\), is the vector space of all linear functionals on \(V\). In other words,
\[
  V' = \mathcal{L}(V,\mathbb{F}).
\]

name: LADR_def_chap_3_112
nl_statement:
If \(v_1,\dots,v_n\) is a basis of \(V\), then the \emph{dual basis} of \(v_1,\dots,v_n\) is the list \(\phi_1,\dots,\phi_n\) of elements of \(V'\), where each \(\phi_j\) is the linear functional on \(V\) such that
\[
  \phi_j(v_k) =
  \begin{cases}
    1 & \text{if } k = j,\\
    0 & \text{if } k \neq j.
  \end{cases}
\]

name: LADR_def_chap_3_118
nl_statement:
Suppose \(T \in \mathcal{L}(V,W)\). The dual map of \(T\) is the linear map \(T' \in \mathcal{L}(W',V')\) defined for each \(\varphi \in W'\) by
\[
  T'(\varphi) = \varphi \circ T.
\]

name: LADR_def_chap_3_121
nl_statement:
For \(U \subseteq V\), the annihilator of \(U\), denoted by \(U^0\), is defined by
\[
  U^0 = \{\varphi \in V' : \varphi(u) = 0 \text{ for all } u \in U\}.
\]

name: LADR_def_chap_4_1
LADR_def_chap_4_2
nl_statement:
Suppose \(z \in \mathbf{C}\).
\begin{itemize}
\item The \emph{complex conjugate} of \(z\in \mathbf{C}\), denoted by \(\overline{z}\), is defined by
\[
  \overline{z} = Re\,z \;-\; (Im\,z)\,i.
\]
\item The \emph{absolute value} of a complex number \(z\), denoted by \(\lvert z\rvert\), is defined by
\[
  \lvert z\rvert = \sqrt{(Re\,z)^2 + (Im\,z)^2}.
\]
\end{itemize}

name: LADR_def_chap_4_5
nl_statement:
A number \(\lambda \in F\) is called a \emph{zero} (or \emph{root}) of a polynomial \(p \in P(F)\) if
\[
  p(\lambda) = 0.
\]

name: LADR_def_chap_5_1
nl_statement: A linear map from a vector space to itself is called an \emph{operator}.

name: LADR_def_chap_5_2
nl_statement: Suppose \(T \in \mathcal{L}(V)\). A subspace \(U\) of \(V\) is called \emph{invariant} under \(T\) if \(T u \in U\) for every \(u \in U\).

name: LADR_def_chap_5_5
nl_statement: Suppose \(T \in \mathcal{L}(V)\). A number \(\lambda \in F\) is called an \emph{eigenvalue} of \(T\) if there exists \(v \in V\) such that \(v \neq 0\) and \(T v = \lambda v\).

name: LADR_def_chap_5_8
nl_statement: Suppose \(T \in \mathcal{L}(V)\) and \(\lambda \in F\) is an eigenvalue of \(T\). A vector \(v \in V\) is called an \emph{eigenvector} of \(T\) corresponding to \(\lambda\) if \(v \neq 0\) and \(T v = \lambda v\).

name: LADR_def_chap_5_16
nl_statement:
If \(p,q \in P(F)\), then \(pq \in P(F)\) is the polynomial defined by
\[
  (pq)(z) = p(z)\,q(z)
\]
for all \(z \in F\).

name: LADR_def_chap_5_21
nl_statement: A \emph{monic polynomial} is a polynomial whose highest-degree coefficient equals \(1\).

name: LADR_def_chap_5_24
nl_statement:
Suppose \(V\) is finite-dimensional and \(T \in \mathcal{L}(V)\). Then the minimal polynomial of \(T\) is the unique monic polynomial \(p \in P(F)\) of smallest degree such that
\[
  p(T) = 0.
\]

name: LADR_def_chap_5_35
nl_statement:
Suppose \(T\in\mathcal{L}(V)\). The matrix of \(T\) with respect to a basis \(v_{1},\dots,v_{n}\) of \(V\) is the \(n\)-by-\(n\) matrix
\[
\mathcal{M}(T)
=
\begin{pmatrix}
A_{1,1} & \dots  & A_{1,n}\\
\vdots  &        & \vdots\\
A_{n,1} & \dots  & A_{n,n}
\end{pmatrix}
\]
whose entries \(A_{j,k}\) are defined by
\[
T v_{k} \;=\; A_{1,k}\,v_{1} \;+\;\dots+\;A_{n,k}\,v_{n}.
\]
The notation \(\mathcal{M}(T,(v_{1},\dots,v_{n}))\) is used if the basis is not clear from the context.

name: LADR_def_chap_5_37
nl_statement: The diagonal of a square matrix consists of the entries on the line from the upper left corner to the bottom right corner.

name: LADR_def_chap_5_38
nl_statement: A square matrix is called \emph{upper triangular} if all entries below the diagonal are $0$.

name: LADR_def_chap_5_48
nl_statement: A diagonal matrix is a square matrix that is 0 everywhere except possibly on the diagonal.

name: LADR_def_chap_5_50
nl_statement: An operator on \(V\) is called diagonalizable if the operator has a diagonal matrix with respect to some basis of \(V\).

name: LADR_def_chap_5_52
nl_statement:
Suppose \(T\in\mathcal{L}(V)\) and \(\lambda\in F\). The eigenspace of \(T\) corresponding to \(\lambda\) is the subspace \(E(\lambda,T)\) of \(V\) defined by
\[
E(\lambda,T)=null(T-\lambda I)=\{v\in V:Tv=\lambda v\}.
\]
Hence \(E(\lambda,T)\) is the set of all eigenvectors of \(T\) corresponding to \(\lambda\), along with the 0 vector.

name: LADR_def_chap_5_66
nl_statement:
Suppose \(T\in\mathcal{L}(V)\) and \(v_{1},\dots,v_{n}\) is a basis of \(V\). Let \(A\) denote the matrix of \(T\) with respect to this basis. A Gershgorin disk of \(T\) with respect to the basis \(v_{1},\dots,v_{n}\) is a set of the form
\[
\bigl\{\,z\in F: \lvert z - A_{j,j}\rvert \le \sum_{\substack{k=1\\k\neq j}}^{n} \lvert A_{j,k}\rvert \bigr\},
\]
where \(j\in\{1,\dots,n\}\).

name: LADR_def_chap_5_71
LADR_def_chap_6_1
nl_statement:
For \(x, y \in \mathbb{R}^n\), the \emph{dot product} of \(x\) and \(y\), denoted by \(x \cdot y\), is defined by
\[
  x \cdot y = x_1 y_1 + \dots + x_n y_n,
\]
where \(x = (x_1, \dots, x_n)\) and \(y = (y_1, \dots, y_n)\).

name: LADR_def_chap_6_2
LADR_def_chap_6_4
nl_statement: An inner product space is a vector space \(V\) along with an inner product on \(V\).

name: LADR_def_chap_6_7
nl_statement:
For \(v \in V\), the \emph{norm} of \(v\), denoted by \(\|v\|\), is defined by
\[
\|v\| = \sqrt{\langle v, v \rangle}.
\]

name: LADR_def_chap_6_10
nl_statement: Two vectors \(u, v \in V\) are called \emph{orthogonal} if \(\langle u, v \rangle = 0\).

name: LADR_def_chap_6_22
LADR_def_chap_6_27
nl_statement: An \emph{orthonormal basis} of \(V\) is an orthonormal list of vectors in \(V\) that is also a basis of \(V\).

name: LADR_def_chap_6_39
LADR_def_chap_6_46
nl_statement:
If \(U\) is a subset of \(V\), then the \emph{orthogonal complement} of \(U\), denoted by \(U^\perp\), is the set of all vectors in \(V\) that are orthogonal to every vector in \(U\):
\[
U^\perp = \{ v \in V : \langle u, v \rangle = 0 \text{ for every } u \in U \}.
\]

name: LADR_def_chap_6_55
nl_statement: Suppose \(U\) is a finite-dimensional subspace of \(V\). The \emph{orthogonal projection} of \(V\) onto \(U\) is the operator \(P_U \in \mathcal{L}(V)\) defined as follows: For each \(v \in V\), write \(v = u + w\), where \(u \in U\) and \(w \in U^\perp\). Then let \(P_U v = u\).

name: LADR_def_chap_6_68
nl_statement:
Suppose that \(V\) is finite-dimensional and \(T \in \mathcal{L}(V, W)\). The \emph{pseudoinverse} \(T^\dagger \in \mathcal{L}(W, V)\) of \(T\) is the linear map from \(W\) to \(V\) defined by
\[
T^\dagger w = \left( T|_{(\text{null } T)^\perp} \right)^{-1} P_{\text{range } T} w
\]
for each \(w \in W\).

name: LADR_def_chap_7_1
nl_statement:
Suppose \(T \in \mathcal{L}(V, W)\). The \emph{adjoint} of \(T\) is the function \(T^* : W \to V\) such that
\[
\langle Tv, w \rangle = \langle v, T^*w \rangle
\]
for every \(v \in V\) and every \(w \in W\).

name: LADR_def_chap_7_7
nl_statement:
The \emph{conjugate transpose} of an \(m\)-by-\(n\) matrix \(A\) is the \(n\)-by-\(m\) matrix \(A^*\) obtained by interchanging the rows and columns and then taking the complex conjugate of each entry. In other words, if \(j \in \{1, \ldots, n\}\) and \(k \in \{1, \ldots, m\}\), then
\[
(A^*)_{j,k} = \overline{A_{k,j}}.
\]

name: LADR_def_chap_7_10
nl_statement: An operator \(T \in \mathcal{L}(V)\) is called \emph{self-adjoint} if \(T = T^*\).

name: LADR_def_chap_7_18
LADR_def_chap_7_34
nl_statement:
An operator \(T \in \mathcal{L}(V)\) is called \emph{positive} if \(T\) is self-adjoint and
\[
\langle Tv, v \rangle \geq 0
\]
for all \(v \in V\).

name: LADR_def_chap_7_36
nl_statement: An operator \(R\) is called a \emph{square root} of an operator \(T\) if \(R^2 = T\).

name: LADR_def_chap_7_44
nl_statement:
A linear map \(S \in \mathcal{L}(V,W)\) is called an \emph{isometry} if
\[
  \|Sv\| = \|v\|
\]
for every \(v \in V\). In other words, a linear map is an isometry if it preserves norms.

name: LADR_def_chap_7_51
nl_statement: An operator \(S \in \mathcal{L}(V)\) is called \emph{unitary} if \(S\) is an invertible isometry.

name: LADR_def_chap_7_56
nl_statement: An \(n\)-by-\(n\) matrix is called \emph{unitary} if its columns form an orthonormal list in \(\mathbb{F}^n\).

name: LADR_def_chap_7_62
nl_statement:
A matrix \(B \in \mathbf{F}^{n,n}\) is called \emph{positive definite} if \(B^* = B\) and
\[
\langle Bx, x \rangle > 0
\]
for every nonzero \(x \in \mathbf{F}^n\).

name: LADR_def_chap_7_65
nl_statement: Suppose \(T \in \mathcal{L}(V, W)\). The \emph{singular values} of \(T\) are the nonnegative square roots of the eigenvalues of \(T^*T\), listed in decreasing order, each included as many times as the dimension of the corresponding eigenspace of \(T^*T\).

name: LADR_def_chap_7_74
nl_statement: An \(M\)-by-\(N\) matrix \(A\) is called a \emph{diagonal matrix} if all entries of the matrix are 0 except possibly \(A_{k,k}\) for \(k = 1, \ldots, \min\{M, N\}\).

name: LADR_def_chap_7_86
nl_statement:
Suppose \(T \in \mathcal{L}(V, W)\). Then the \emph{norm} of \(T\), denoted by \(\|T\|\), is defined by
\[
\|T\| = \max\{ \|Tv\| : v \in V \text{ and } \|v\| \leq 1 \}.
\]

name: LADR_def_chap_7_95
nl_statement:
The \emph{ball} in \(V\) of radius 1 centered at 0, denoted by \(B\), is defined by
\[
B = \{ v \in V : \|v\| < 1 \}.
\]

name: LADR_def_chap_7_96
nl_statement:
Suppose that \(f_1, \ldots, f_n\) is an orthonormal basis of \(V\) and \(s_1, \ldots, s_n\) are positive numbers. The \emph{ellipsoid} \(E(s_1f_1, \ldots, s_nf_n)\) with \emph{principal axes} \(s_1f_1, \ldots, s_nf_n\) is defined by
\[
E(s_1f_1, \ldots, s_nf_n) = \left\{ v \in V : \frac{|\langle v, f_1 \rangle|^2}{s_1^2} + \cdots + \frac{|\langle v, f_n \rangle|^2}{s_n^2} < 1 \right\}.
\]

name: LADR_def_chap_7_102
nl_statement:
Suppose \(v_1, \ldots, v_n\) is a basis of \(V\). Let
\[
P(v_1, \ldots, v_n) = \{ a_1v_1 + \cdots + a_nv_n : a_1, \ldots, a_n \in (0,1) \}.
\]
A \emph{parallelepiped} is a set of the form \(u + P(v_1, \ldots, v_n)\) for some \(u \in V\). The vectors \(v_1, \ldots, v_n\) are called the \emph{edges} of this parallelepiped.

name: LADR_def_chap_7_105
nl_statement:
A \emph{box} in \(V\) is a set of the form
\[
u + P(r_1e_1, \ldots, r_ne_n),
\]
where \(u \in V\) and \(r_1, \ldots, r_n\) are positive numbers and \(e_1, \ldots, e_n\) is an orthonormal basis of \(V\).

name: LADR_def_chap_7_108
nl_statement:
Suppose \(\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{R}\). If \(u \in V\) and \(r_1, \ldots, r_n\) are positive numbers and \(e_1, \ldots, e_n\) is an orthonormal basis of \(V\), then
\[
\text{volume}(u + P(r_1e_1, \ldots, r_ne_n)) = r_1 \times \cdots \times r_n.
\]

name: LADR_def_chap_7_109
nl_statement: Suppose \(\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{R}\) and \(\Omega \subseteq V\). Then the \emph{volume} of \(\Omega\), denoted by \(\text{volume } \Omega\), is approximately the sum of the volumes of a collection of disjoint boxes that approximate \(\Omega\).

name: LADR_def_chap_8_8
nl_statement:
Suppose \(T \in \mathcal{L}(V)\) and \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(T\). A vector \(v \in V\) is called a \emph{generalized eigenvector} of \(T\) corresponding to \(\lambda\) if \(v \ne 0\) and
\[
(T - \lambda I)^k v = 0
\]
for some positive integer \(k\).

name: LADR_def_chap_8_14
nl_statement: An operator is called \emph{nilpotent} if some power of it equals 0.

name: LADR_def_chap_8_19
nl_statement:
Suppose \(T \in \mathcal{L}(V)\) and \(\lambda \in \mathbb{F}\). The \emph{generalized eigenspace} of \(T\) corresponding to \(\lambda\), denoted by \(G(\lambda, T)\), is defined by
\[
G(\lambda, T) = \{ v \in V : (T - \lambda I)^k v = 0 \text{ for some positive integer } k \}.
\]
Thus \(G(\lambda, T)\) is the set of generalized eigenvectors of \(T\) corresponding to \(\lambda\), along with the 0 vector.

name: LADR_def_chap_8_23
nl_statement:
\begin{itemize}
  \item Suppose \(T \in \mathcal{L}(V)\). The \emph{multiplicity} of an eigenvalue \(\lambda\) of \(T\) is defined to be the dimension of the corresponding generalized eigenspace \(G(\lambda, T)\).
  \item In other words, the multiplicity of an eigenvalue \(\lambda\) of \(T\) equals
  \[
  \dim \text{ null }(T - \lambda I)^{\dim V}.
  \]
\end{itemize}

name: LADR_def_chap_8_26
nl_statement:
Suppose \(\mathbb{F} = \mathbb{C}\) and \(T \in \mathcal{L}(V)\). Let \(\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_m\) denote the distinct eigenvalues of \(T\), with multiplicities \(d_1, \ldots, d_m\). The polynomial
\[
(z - \lambda_1)^{d_1} \cdots (z - \lambda_m)^{d_m}
\]
is called the \emph{characteristic polynomial} of \(T\).

name: LADR_def_chap_8_35
nl_statement:
A \emph{block diagonal matrix} is a square matrix of the form
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
A_1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
0 & \ddots & & \vdots \\
\vdots & & \ddots & 0 \\
0 & \cdots & 0 & A_m
\end{pmatrix},
\]
where \(A_1, \ldots, A_m\) are square matrices lying along the diagonal and all other entries of the matrix equal 0.

name: LADR_def_chap_8_44
nl_statement:
Suppose \(T \in \mathcal{L}(V)\). A basis of \(V\) is called a \emph{Jordan basis} for \(T\) if with respect to this basis \(T\) has a block diagonal matrix
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
A_1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
0 & \ddots & \ddots & \vdots \\
\vdots & \ddots & \ddots & 0 \\
0 & \cdots & 0 & A_p
\end{pmatrix},
\]
in which each \(A_k\) is an upper-triangular matrix of the form
\[
A_k =
\begin{pmatrix}
\lambda_k & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
0 & \lambda_k & 1 & \ddots & \vdots \\
\vdots & \ddots & \ddots & \ddots & 0 \\
\vdots & & \ddots & \lambda_k & 1 \\
0 & \cdots & \cdots & 0 & \lambda_k
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

name: LADR_def_chap_8_47
nl_statement: Suppose \(A\) is a square matrix with entries in \(F\). The \emph{trace} of \(A\), denoted \(\mathrm{tr}A\), is defined to be the sum of the diagonal entries of \(A\).

name: LADR_def_chap_8_51
nl_statement:
Suppose \(T \in \mathcal{L}(V)\). The \emph{trace} of \(T\), denoted \(\mathrm{tr}\, T\), is defined by
\[
\mathrm{tr}\, T = \mathrm{tr}\, \mathcal{M}(T, (v_1, \ldots, v_n)),
\]
where \(v_1, \ldots, v_n\) is any basis of \(V\).

name: LADR_def_chap_9_1
nl_statement:
A \emph{bilinear form} on \(V\) is a function \(\beta\colon V \times V \to F\) such that the maps
\[
  v \mapsto \beta(v,u)
  \quad\text{and}\quad
  v \mapsto \beta(u,v)
\]
are both linear functionals on \(V\) for every \(u \in V\).

name: LADR_def_chap_9_3
nl_statement: The set of bilinear forms on \(V\) is denoted by \(V^{(2)}\).

name: LADR_def_chap_9_4
nl_statement:
Suppose \(\beta\) is a bilinear form on \(V\) and \(e_1, \ldots, e_n\) is a basis of \(V\). The \emph{matrix} of \(\beta\) with respect to this basis is the \(n\)-by-\(n\) matrix \(\mathcal{M}(\beta)\) whose entry \(\mathcal{M}(\beta)_{j,k}\) in row \(j\), column \(k\) is given by
\[
\mathcal{M}(\beta)_{j,k} = \beta(e_j, e_k).
\]
If the basis \(e_1, \ldots, e_n\) is not clear from the context, then the notation \(\mathcal{M}(\beta, (e_1, \ldots, e_n))\) is used.

name: LADR_def_chap_9_9
nl_statement:
A bilinear form \(\rho \in V^{(2)}\) is called \emph{symmetric} if
\[
\rho(u, w) = \rho(w, u)
\]
for all \(u, w \in V\). The set of symmetric bilinear forms on \(V\) is denoted by \(V^{(2)}_{\text{sym}}\).

name: LADR_def_chap_9_11
nl_statement: A square matrix \(A\) is called \emph{symmetric} if it equals its transpose.

name: LADR_def_chap_9_14
nl_statement:
A bilinear form \(\alpha \in V^{(2)}\) is called \emph{alternating} if
\[
\alpha(v, v) = 0
\]
for all \(v \in V\). The set of alternating bilinear forms on \(V\) is denoted by \(V^{(2)}_{\text{alt}}\).

name: LADR_def_chap_9_18
nl_statement:
For \(\beta\) a bilinear form on \(V\), define a function \(q_\beta : V \to \mathbb{F}\) by \(q_\beta(v) = \beta(v, v)\).  
A function \(q : V \to \mathbb{F}\) is called a \emph{quadratic form} on \(V\) if there exists a bilinear form \(\beta\) on \(V\) such that \(q = q_\beta\).

name: LADR_def_chap_9_24
nl_statement:
For \(m\) a positive integer, define \(V^m\) by
\[
V^m = \underbrace{V \times \cdots \times V}_{m \text{ times}}.
\]

name: LADR_def_chap_9_25
nl_statement:
\begin{itemize}
  \item For \(m\) a positive integer, an \(m\)-\emph{linear form} on \(V\) is a function \(\beta : V^m \to \mathbb{F}\) that is linear in each slot when the other slots are held fixed. This means that for each \(k \in \{1, \ldots, m\}\) and all \(u_1, \ldots, u_m \in V\), the function
  \[
  v \mapsto \beta(u_1, \ldots, u_{k-1}, v, u_{k+1}, \ldots, u_m)
  \]
  is a linear map from \(V\) to \(\mathbb{F}\).

  \item The set of \(m\)-linear forms on \(V\) is denoted by \(V^{(m)}\).

  \item A function \(\beta\) is called a \emph{multilinear form} on \(V\) if it is an \(m\)-linear form on \(V\) for some positive integer \(m\).
\end{itemize}

name: LADR_def_chap_9_27
LADR_def_chap_9_31
LADR_def_chap_9_32
nl_statement:
The \emph{sign} of a permutation \((j_1, \ldots, j_m)\) is defined by
\[
\text{sign}(j_1, \ldots, j_m) = (-1)^N,
\]
where \(N\) is the number of pairs of integers \((k, \ell)\) with \(1 \leq k < \ell \leq m\) such that \(k\) appears after \(\ell\) in the list \((j_1, \ldots, j_m)\).

name: LADR_def_chap_9_40
nl_statement:
Suppose that \(m\) is a positive integer and \(T \in \mathcal{L}(V)\). For \(\alpha \in V^{(m)}_{\text{alt}}\), define \(\alpha_T \in V^{(m)}_{\text{alt}}\) by
\[
\alpha_T(v_1, \ldots, v_m) = \alpha(Tv_1, \ldots, Tv_m)
\]
for each list \(v_1, \ldots, v_m\) of vectors in \(V\).

name: LADR_def_chap_9_41
nl_statement:
Suppose \(T \in \mathcal{L}(V)\). The \emph{determinant} of \(T\), denoted by \(\det T\), is defined to be the unique number in \(\mathbf{F}\) such that
\[
\alpha_T = (\det T)\, \alpha
\]
for all \(\alpha \in V^{(\dim V)}_{\text{alt}}\).

name: LADR_def_chap_9_43
nl_statement: Suppose that \(n\) is a positive integer and \(A\) is an \(n\)-by-\(n\) square matrix with entries in \(\mathbb{F}\). Let \(T \in \mathcal{L}(\mathbb{F}^n)\) be the operator whose matrix with respect to the standard basis of \(\mathbb{F}^n\) equals \(A\). The \emph{determinant} of \(A\), denoted by \(\det A\), is defined by \(\det A = \det T\).

name: LADR_def_chap_9_63
nl_statement:
Suppose \(T \in \mathcal{L}(V)\). The polynomial defined by
\[
z \mapsto \det(zI - T)
\]
is called the \emph{characteristic polynomial} of \(T\).

name: LADR_def_chap_9_68
LADR_def_chap_9_71
nl_statement:
\begin{itemize}
  \item The \emph{tensor product} \(V \otimes W\) is defined to be \(\mathcal{B}(V', W')\).
  \item For \(v \in V\) and \(w \in W\), the \emph{tensor product} \(v \otimes w\) is the element of \(V \otimes W\) defined by
  \[
  (v \otimes w)(\varphi, \tau) = \varphi(v)\, \tau(w)
  \]
  for all \((\varphi, \tau) \in V' \times W'\).
\end{itemize}

name: LADR_def_chap_9_77
nl_statement: A \emph{bilinear map} from \(V \times W\) to a vector space \(U\) is a function \(\Gamma : V \times W \to U\) such that \(v \mapsto \Gamma(v, w)\) is a linear map from \(V\) to \(U\) for each \(w \in W\) and \(w \mapsto \Gamma(v, w)\) is a linear map from \(W\) to \(U\) for each \(v \in V\).

name: LADR_def_chap_9_82
nl_statement:
Suppose \(V\) and \(W\) are inner product spaces. The inner product on \(V \otimes W\) is the unique function \(\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle\) from \((V \otimes W) \times (V \otimes W)\) to \(\mathbb{F}\) such that
\[
\langle v \otimes w, u \otimes x \rangle = \langle v, u \rangle \langle w, x \rangle
\]
for all \(v, u \in V\) and \(w, x \in W\).

name: LADR_def_chap_9_85
LADR_def_chap_9_88
nl_statement:
\begin{itemize}
  \item The \emph{tensor product} \(V_1 \otimes \cdots \otimes V_m\) is defined to be \(\mathcal{B}(V_1', \ldots, V_m')\).
  \item For \(v_1 \in V_1, \ldots, v_m \in V_m\), the \emph{tensor product} \(v_1 \otimes \cdots \otimes v_m\) is the element of \(V_1 \otimes \cdots \otimes V_m\) defined by
  \[
    (v_1 \otimes \cdots \otimes v_m)(\varphi_1, \ldots, \varphi_m) = \varphi_1(v_1) \cdots \varphi_m(v_m)
  \]
  for all \((\varphi_1, \ldots, \varphi_m) \in V_1' \times \cdots \times V_m'\).
\end{itemize}

name: LADR_def_chap_9_91
nl_statement: An \emph{m-linear map} from \(V_1 \times \cdots \times V_m\) to a vector space \(U\) is a function \(\Gamma : V_1 \times \cdots \times V_m \to U\) that is a linear map in each slot when the other slots are held fixed.
